text: So this was a lady who was undergoing a left upper lobe lobectomy and she'd had some immunotherapy and in dissecting the truncus they encountered some bleeding.
So a difficult spot to fix.
difficult spot to expose, and she had quite a bit of inflammation around the hilum.
So the first thing was to obtain control.
And this is generally best done by just applying direct pressure.
The nice thing about the robot is that once you put pressure on something and then lock the arm, it doesn't move.
So direct pressure was applied for several minutes and it seemed to stop the bleeding at least enough that further exposure could be achieved.
So the lingular bronchi were in the way and so the first thing was to dissect out those airways and this is the
inferior lingular bronchus, the B5 segmental bronchus.
And this is probably the artery to the superior segment, or S4.
And then the superior segment, or the B4 segment, is being divided.
So now that's going to expose the truncus a little bit better.
And although it looked like the bleeding had temporarily stopped, it was clear that there was ongoing bleeding from that branch.
So additional pressure.
We considered opening at this point.
However, there was excellent visualization.
And so we decided that we would at least attempt to control the PA proximally.
So the fourth arm is putting pressure on the truncus and preventing major blood loss.
And then we're dissecting on the top side and underneath the main pulmonary artery.
And actually the visualization in this case is probably even better than what I could get with a thoracotomy.
So we're going to pass a vessel or an umbilical tape around the proximal main pulmonary artery.
Now before we did any of this, of course, we made sure that the patient was adequately resuscitated, that there was blood available in the room.
and that the anesthesiologists were present and we had good IV access.
So the umbilical tape was passed around the main pulmonary artery and I just placed a vessel loop around the inferior pulmonary vein just to help with backflow bleeding.
So now we used an endoscopic vascular clamp that was placed through the assist trocar.
There were small branches, probably to the anterior segment, that we divided with a clip as shown.
And that then left us with the bronchus to the trisegment, the S1 plus 2 and 3.
And so that was then completely divided.
You can see even with the clamp applied that there's still a fair bit of black bleeding.
So we could see the defect, and this was initially closed just with a figervate suture 5-0 proline.
And this helped a lot in slowing things down.
So there was one small additional branch and it was actually at the base of this branch off of the truncus where the injury appeared to be.
So this was just tied and then the
branch cut distally to it.
So now at this point I placed a pledged suture of 5-0 proline and here you have to modify the pledged sutures a little bit.
These are double armed as you know and so you can see the pledged that normally would have a lot more suture length applied to it having been tied the suture was essentially abbreviated
So the whole plagiatus suture was approximately 20 centimeters or so.
So that was then tied down just to provide a little additional security.
And then we released the aortic clamp.
So not every pulmonary artery bleed requires opening.
And certainly in this situation, it was deemed safe enough to be able to obtain proximal and distal control and to repair the vessel robotically.
